entries.title: Why Lettuce Is Always Making People Sick
entries.summary: The cyclospora diarrhea outbreak isn’t an isolated incident. It’s part of a pattern of leafy greens wreaking digestive havoc.
entries.content:
Salad is usually synonymous with healthy eating. Except now, as the US grapples with an expanding outbreak of cyclosporiasis that’s sickened more than 7,100 people in Michigan alone, likely caused by iceberg lettuce supplied by Taylor Farms.

In fact, leafy greens are frequently implicated in instances of foodborne illness. There was a 2022 multistate E. coli outbreak tied to romaine served on Wendy’s sandwiches and 173 cases of norovirus linked to Illinois eatery D.C. Cobb’s. In 2021, BrightFarms recalled packaged salad greens sold in several states after 31 people got salmonella. And in 2019, a total of 167 people were infected with E. coli after eating romaine harvested from the Salinas Valley region in California. This isn't even the first cyclospora outbreak linked to lettuce: In 2020, Fresh Express salad kits were linked to more than 700 cases of people afflicted with the parasite.

The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention estimates that produce is responsible for almost half of the reported foodborne illness cases, with leafy vegetables topping the list. So what is it that makes lettuce a major source of America’s foodborne tummy troubles?

One big reason is simply because lots of people eat it. Lettuce is the second-most-consumed vegetable in the US behind potatoes. (The latest outbreak ensures potatoes will remain the king of US veggies. Plus, french fries.) Wraps, subs, tacos, you name a fast food sandwich and it’s almost certainly got lettuce on it. Salad-slinging chains are fixtures across the US. Grocery store coolers are stocked with salad kits, on top of the unadorned heads of iceberg lettuce and containers of spinach.

Those ready-to-eat salads and blends of spring mix pose a particularly acute risk because they contain multiple types of leafy greens, which can come from different farms and locations. If one type of lettuce in the mix is contaminated, it can contaminate the whole bag. And because that contaminated lettuce gets packaged into lots and lots of bagged salad, it has the potential to spread germs far and wide.

How lettuce is grown can also expose it to a wide array of pathogens. A 2025 study by researchers at Cornell University who studied E. coli outbreaks in romaine lettuce found that a lot of contamination originates from irrigation with untreated surface water applied through overhead spray irrigation systems. There are ways to lower that risk, including switching to irrigation systems that do not directly touch the leaves, but that would require upgrades and downtime.

Lettuce’s satisfying crunch is also what makes it prone to pesky pathogens. Since it’s eaten raw—really, who wants wilted lettuce?—there’s no opportunity to cook off any lingering contaminants. Like other raw fruit and produce, leafy greens don’t go through what’s known as a “kill step”—heating, pasteurization, or freezing—which is designed to eliminate pathogens during the food manufacturing process.

The texture of lettuce is an additional factor in its ability to spread disease.“Many lettuce types have a lot of crevices and folds,” says Jeanne Marrazzo, CEO of the Infectious Diseases Society of America. “It’s very easy for these tiny pathogens to get in there.”

Even prewashing procedures at manufacturing facilities don’t always remove microbes, which can cling tightly to leaves. In the case of cyclospora, the parasite has thick outer walls, making it highly resistant to most food sanitizers. Experts say vigorous washing that involves scrubbing is the best way to get rid of cyclospora.

“If you don't wash these products well, you're going to end up ingesting whatever is on the lettuce,” says Amira Roess, an epidemiologist at George Mason University.

entries.title: The White House Is Trying to Figure Out What to Do About Chinese AI
entries.summary: There’s a debate going on in the Trump administration over how to handle increasingly powerful Chinese AI models.
entries.content:
The Trump administration is split over how to respond to the rapid rise of China’s leading AI models, according to multiple people familiar with deliberations.

The debate is broadly divided between parts of the White House, which has pushed for stricter controls on Chinese AI that may soon rival the most powerful US models, and the Commerce Department, which has viewed those restrictions as unworkable.

The conundrum has become the latest flash point for the administration over AI, after China’s Moonshot AI lab last week released its Kimi K3 model that rivals the top US models from Anthropic and OpenAI.

In recent days, senior White House officials have discussed possible measures to stop China’s AI labs from developing their models by training them on US models, a practice known as distillation, the people say.

The White House is expected to take some sort of presidential action after Anthropic last month alleged the Chinese tech company Alibaba had carried out the “largest known distillation attack” against it to date. This action is not currently expected to take the form of an executive order, according to one of the people.

A White House spokesperson did not comment on the reporting in this story.

The White House has been taking distillation attacks especially seriously. It announced on Wednesday that Moonshot had developed Kimi K3 by distilling Anthropic’s Claude Fable 5 model, and Treasury secretary Scott Bessent told Fox Business that covert distillation could lead to sanctions, describing it as “IP theft.”

While proposals have been making the rounds inside the White House, no formal request for input has been transmitted yet to the Commerce Department, which has its own ideas, according to two people familiar with deliberations.

The Commerce Department has increasingly emerged as an influential part of the intra-administration split over AI, in part because it oversees export controls through the Bureau of Industry and Security.

Lutnick has contemplated ways to create incentives for top US labs to create their own open weight models to counterbalance China, and has spoken with leaders at a number of AI labs in recent weeks, the people add.

Lutnick appears to be straddling a middle ground of regulation—he imposed export controls on Anthropic to bring them to heel—but has been more freewheeling than Cairncross, one of the people said.

Earlier this month, the biggest AI issue for the administration was Anthropic’s Claude Mythos and Fable 5 models, because of their ability to find vulnerabilities in government systems and critical infrastructure.

But policy headaches with AI have only escalated with the rise of so-called open weight models from China—models that have few to no safeguards to prevent hacking government systems and can be downloaded and used by anyone.

This is an edition of Hugo Lowell’s Inner Loop newsletter. Read previous newsletters here.

entries.title: Top Red-Light Therapy Deals at Nordstrom’s Anniversary Sale (2026)
entries.summary: Save on WIRED-tested LED therapy devices from TheraFace, HigherDose, and more.
entries.content:
Does LED therapy really work? There's plenty of scientific evidence in its favor, but will that translate to actual, visible results you can see in the mirror? Members of WIRED's Reviews team have spent the past year and a half testing all the popular masks, hats, and helmets to see for ourselves which devices are worth your money. Luckily, some of our favorites are on rare discount during Nordstrom's annual Anniversary Sale, which runs through August 9.

For more in-depth information on our tests, check out our guide to the Best Red Light Therapy Masks and Best Red Light Therapy for Hair Growth.

Dr. Dennis Gross Spectralite FaceWare Pro (Red) for $340 ($115 Off)

This is an excellent price on a mask that isn't often on sale. I own this mask and highly recommend it due to its short treatment time. Many red-light therapy masks require 10-minute sessions several times a week, but with this model, you only need three minutes a day. Given that skin needs to be totally clean—that means free of makeup and skin-care—to use any LED mask, those longer treatment times can be disruptive to your typical morning or nighttime routine. That’s not a problem with the SpectraLite. Note that the sale is only on the red color, not the rose gold shown above.

HigherDose Red Light Hat for $359 ($90 Off)

After months of trying all manner of LED devices for hair growth, many of our testers have been impressed with their results. One of the biggest hits was this hat from HigherDose. It looks and wears like a normal baseball cap, and WIRED reviewer Nena Farrell saw tons of new growth after her trichotillomania-related hair loss. Just note that these devices are designed to stimulate dormant or weakened hair follicles—they will not perform miracles on follicles that have died due to aging, alopecia, or other damaging conditions.

TheraFace Mask Glo for $300 ($80 Off)

When our tester tried this hard-shell mask for our guide to LED Face Masks, she didn't love it as much as the original TheraFace mask. (The older edition is unfortunately not on sale.) This updated model moved TheraBody's signature vibration feature to the mask straps, rather than the mask itself, which our tester didn’t love. But it’s still a solid option and a rare sale if you're interested in an LED mask that offers massage.

Omnilux Contour LED Face Mask: Gift With Purchase ($29 Value)

Some people prefer a flexible silicone mask as opposed to the hard-shell style. I personally don't think it's as comfortable, but it is inarguably easier to store and pack for travel. This Nordstrom-exclusive model features 633 nanometers (nm) and 830 nm of near-infrared light, which is on par with most other flat masks. It only has one mode—there's no blue-light acne-targeting option or other wavelengths—and the treatments are 10 minutes long (recommended three to five times a week). There's no discount on the Contour during the Anniversary Sale, but it does come with a bonus gift of three hyaluronic acid Hydrogel Facial Masks ($29).
